VSCode's built-in "Download..." for the Explorer isn't built for large files — it streams through the same protocol as file editing (chunked over the remote connection, no parallelism, no resume). For 3.7GB that's slow almost no matter what. Direct `scp`/`rsync` from a real terminal is much faster and — importantly — resumable if it drops.

content.text:
**Do this instead — open a terminal on your own laptop** (not the VSCode remote terminal, your actual local one), and run:

```bash
rsync -avP janlud@hpclogin1:/work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR/genomes.tar.gz ~/Downloads/
```

(If `hpclogin1` isn't directly reachable from outside DTU's network and you normally connect through a specific SSH config/jump host or VPN, use whatever hostname you already use for Remote-SSH in VSCode — check `~/.ssh/config` on your laptop for the `Host` entry, or just swap in the same address you used to connect VSCode in the first place.)

Why this is faster:
- **`-P`** shows live progress and, critically, lets you resume if the connection drops — restart the same command and it picks up where it left off instead of starting over.
- **`-a`** preserves file integrity (checksums after transfer).
- It runs directly over SSH with no editor-protocol overhead, so it saturates your actual link bandwidth instead of being bottlenecked by VSCode's remote-file-access layer.

If `rsync` isn't available on your machine (rare, but happens on some Windows setups without WSL), plain `scp` works too, just without resume:
```bash
scp janlud@hpclogin1:/work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR/genomes.tar.gz ~/Downloads/
```

If it's still slow after switching to this, it's a real network bandwidth ceiling (either DTU's egress or your local connection) rather than something fixable on the transfer-method side.

hpclogin1(janlud) $ scp janlud@hpclogin1:/work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR/genomes.tar.gz ~/Downloads/

When connecting from _outside_ of DTU (and not using DTU-VPN) one is 
required to authenticate with a ssh-key + ssh-key-keyphrase + DTU-password.

From inside of DTU (or connecting via DTU-VPN) one can also connect 
with password or ssh-key.

Info page on SSH: https://www.hpc.dtu.dk/?page_id=4317

---
For ThinLinc users: 
Please make sure that your TL client is at least version 4.16 - else you 
cannot connect to this system!

This is not an error-message, it's just a way to give you some information. 
Click 'Close' to continue ...
---

Info page on the system update 08/2024: https://www.hpc.dtu.dk/?page_id=5209

---

scp: open local "/zhome/1d/c/234360/Downloads/": Is a directory
/work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR
hpclogin1(janlud) $ 
did it work?

No — that ran **on the HPC machine itself**, not on your laptop. Look at the prompt: it says `hpclogin1(janlud) $` both before and after — meaning you're already inside a remote session on `hpclogin1`, and running `scp janlud@hpclogin1:...` from there just connected the machine to itself in a loop. `~/Downloads/` then resolved to `/zhome/1d/c/234360/Downloads/` (your HPC home directory), not your laptop's Downloads folder — and it errored out anyway because that directory already exists there. **Nothing was transferred to your computer.**

The key thing: if you opened this terminal through VSCode's Remote-SSH window (or ThinLinc), that terminal is *running on the remote HPC machine*, even though the VSCode window is on your screen. It's not your local terminal.

**What you actually need:** a completely separate terminal application, opened on your laptop, *outside* of VSCode/ThinLinc entirely:
- **Mac:** open Terminal.app or iTerm
- **Windows:** open PowerShell, or WSL if you have it
- **Linux:** your regular terminal

From *that* terminal (which will not show any `hpclogin1` prompt until you actually connect), run:

```bash
rsync -avP janlud@hpclogin1.gbar.dtu.dk:/work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR/genomes.tar.gz ~/Downloads/
```

(I added `.gbar.dtu.dk` since that's DTU HPC's standard external hostname — adjust if you normally use something else to connect.)

Based on the banner you posted, expect this auth flow:
- **If you're outside DTU's network / not on DTU VPN:** you'll need an SSH key + your key's passphrase + your DTU password (multi-step, all prompted interactively).
- **If you're inside DTU's network or on DTU VPN:** plain password or SSH key works.

If `rsync` isn't found on your machine, `scp` works the same way as a fallback — just without resume support.

(base) janikludwig@mbaJL ~ % rsync -avP janlud@hpclogin1.gbar.dtu.dk:/work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR/genomes.tar.gz ~/Downloads/

ssh: Could not resolve hostname hpclogin1.gbar.dtu.dk: nodename nor servname provided, or not known
rsync: connection unexpectedly closed (0 bytes received so far) [receiver]
rsync error: unexplained error (code 255) at /AppleInternal/Library/BuildRoots/5bb93434-bef0-11ef-bef2-d285688f7a47/Library/Caches/com.apple.xbs/Sources/rsync/rsync/io.c(453) [receiver=2.6.9]
(base) janikludwig@mbaJL ~ %

Good — that prompt confirms you're actually on your Mac now. But I guessed at `hpclogin1.gbar.dtu.dk` and got it wrong — I shouldn't have invented that hostname. Let's use the one that's already working for your VSCode Remote-SSH connection, since that one definitely resolves correctly.

On your Mac (same terminal), check your SSH config:

```bash
cat ~/.ssh/config
```

Look for a `Host` entry that references the HPC (something with `hpclogin`, `dtu`, or similar in it, possibly using a `ProxyJump`/`ProxyCommand` if you connect through a gateway). Paste me what you find (feel free to redact anything sensitive) and I'll give you the exact working command — either the real hostname, or if VSCode connects through an SSH alias, we can just use that alias name directly in the `rsync` command instead of typing out the full hostname.

ost dtu-hpc
    HostName login1.hpc.dtu.dk
    User janlud
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/dtu_hpc

The alias is `dtu-hpc`, and the real hostname is `login1.hpc.dtu.dk` (not what I guessed). Since it's already a configured `Host` alias, just use the alias name directly — SSH/rsync will pick up the `HostName`, `User`, and `IdentityFile` automatically:

```bash
rsync -avP dtu-hpc:/work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR/genomes.tar.gz ~/Downloads/
```

That should connect using your existing key (`~/.ssh/dtu_hpc`) exactly the way VSCode does, prompt for your key passphrase (and DTU password too, if you're off-network/VPN per the earlier banner), and then start streaming the 3.7GB file with a live progress bar. If it drops partway, just rerun the same command — `-P` will resume rather than restart.

lastPrompt: ost dtu-hpc     HostName login1.hpc.dtu.dk     User janlud     IdentityFile ~/.ssh/dtu_hpc
